title: AI in Design Systems
description: Learn to integrate AI components into your design system — from documentation generation to pattern libraries to accessibility auditing with Claude.
learningObjectives: Design and document AI-specific components within a design system, including loading states, confidence indicators, and feedback mechanisms
Use Claude to generate, maintain, and audit design system documentation including usage guidelines, code examples, and accessibility notes
Apply AI-powered accessibility auditing techniques to identify and resolve compliance issues across design system components

sections.title: AI Components in Design Systems
sections.content:
As AI features become standard in digital products, design systems need to evolve to include components specifically designed for AI interactions. These components are fundamentally different from traditional UI components because they need to handle non-deterministic content, variable latency, and uncertainty — states that most design systems were never built to address.

**Why AI Components Need Their Own Category**

Existing design system components assume predictable behavior: a button click triggers a known action, a form field accepts defined input types, a card displays structured data. AI components break these assumptions:

• **Non-deterministic output** — An AI-generated response component needs to handle text of unpredictable length, structure, and format. Unlike a product card that always has a title, image, and price, an AI response might be a single sentence or a multi-paragraph analysis with bullet points, code blocks, and tables.
• **Variable timing** — AI processing takes anywhere from milliseconds to 30+ seconds depending on the task. Components need built-in loading, streaming, and timeout states that traditional components don't require.
• **Confidence and uncertainty** — AI outputs carry varying degrees of reliability. Components need ways to communicate confidence levels, source attribution, and verification status — concepts that don't exist in traditional UI.

**Essential AI Component Types**

Every design system supporting AI features should include these component categories:

• **AI response containers** — Components that display AI-generated content with appropriate labeling, formatting support, and action buttons (copy, regenerate, edit, expand/collapse). These containers should visually distinguish AI content from human content through subtle background colors, border treatments, or iconography.
• **Streaming text components** — Components that display text as it's generated, character by character or word by word. These need cursor indicators, smooth animation, and the ability to transition from streaming to complete states without layout shifts.
• **Confidence indicators** — Visual elements that communicate how certain the AI is about its output. These range from simple labels ("High confidence" / "Needs review") to color-coded confidence bars to source citation badges.
• **Feedback mechanisms** — Standardized ways for users to rate AI outputs. Thumbs up/down buttons, satisfaction scales, "Report inaccuracy" links, and optional text feedback fields. These should be consistent across all AI features in the product.
• **AI loading states** — Specialized loading components that go beyond spinners: skeleton screens shaped to the expected output, progress indicators with contextual labels ("Analyzing your document..."), and cancelation affordances.

**Designing for AI Component Variants**

Each AI component needs more variants than traditional components:

• **State coverage** — Map out every possible state: empty (no AI interaction yet), loading (AI is processing), streaming (content arriving in real time), complete (full response available), error (generation failed), timeout (took too long), and degraded (partial result available). Most traditional components have 3-4 states. AI components often need 7+.
• **Content adaptability** — AI response containers must handle plain text, markdown, code, tables, lists, and embedded media. Test with the shortest and longest realistic outputs to ensure the component works at both extremes.
• **Contextual variations** — The same AI feedback component might appear inline in a chat interface, in a sidebar panel, or in a modal. Design variants for each context with appropriate sizing and layout behavior.

sections.title: Generating and Maintaining Documentation with Claude
sections.content:
Design system documentation is essential but notoriously underfunded. Teams build components but skip the documentation that makes them usable by other designers and developers. Claude can dramatically lower the cost of creating and maintaining high-quality documentation.

**Component Documentation Generation**

For each component in your design system, Claude can draft comprehensive documentation if you provide it with the component's specifications:

• **Usage guidelines** — Describe the component's purpose and constraints to Claude, and ask it to generate a usage guide that covers: when to use this component, when not to use it (and what to use instead), and common misuse patterns to avoid. "This is a toast notification component. Generate usage guidelines covering when to use toasts vs. banners vs. inline alerts, maximum content length, and auto-dismiss timing."
• **Props and API documentation** — For developer-facing documentation, provide Claude with the component's props or API interface and ask it to generate descriptions for each prop, including type, default value, and a usage example. Claude is particularly good at generating code examples that show props in context.
• **Do/Don't examples** — Ask Claude to generate pairs of correct and incorrect usage examples. "For this dropdown component, generate 5 do/don't pairs covering: label clarity, option count, default selection, keyboard accessibility, and error states." These examples are the fastest way for designers to understand component usage.

**Pattern Documentation**

Beyond individual components, design systems need documentation for how components work together:

• **Composition patterns** — Ask Claude to describe how components combine to create common UI patterns. "Document how the search input, filter chip, results list, and pagination components combine to create a search experience. Include the interaction flow, state management between components, and responsive behavior."
• **Layout guidelines** — Generate documentation for spacing, alignment, and grid usage with Claude. Provide your spacing scale and grid specifications, and ask Claude to write guidelines with visual descriptions of correct and incorrect spacing in common layouts.
• **Content templates** — Ask Claude to generate content templates for each component that requires text. A modal dialog template might specify: title format (verb + noun, 5 words max), body text guidelines (one paragraph, explain the action and consequences), and button label conventions (specific action verb, never "OK").

**Maintaining Documentation Over Time**

The hardest part of design system documentation isn't writing it — it's keeping it current:

• **Change-driven updates** — When a component is updated, describe the changes to Claude along with the existing documentation. Ask it to update the documentation to reflect the changes, flag any sections that might be affected by the change, and highlight any new usage guidelines needed.
• **Consistency audits** — Periodically feed all your component documentation to Claude and ask it to identify inconsistencies: different terminology for the same concepts, conflicting guidelines between components, or missing sections that exist in other component docs.
• **Gap identification** — Ask Claude to review your design system documentation and identify missing documentation. "Here's a list of our 40 components and which ones have complete documentation. For the 12 components without docs, prioritize them by how frequently they're used and how complex they are."

sections.title: Accessibility Auditing with AI
sections.content:
Accessibility is a requirement, not a feature, but it's often the last thing teams think about. Claude can serve as an always-available accessibility reviewer that catches issues before they reach users, helping teams build accessible components from the start rather than remediating after the fact.

**Component-Level Accessibility Review**

Every design system component should meet WCAG 2.1 AA standards at minimum. Claude can help audit each component:

• **Color contrast analysis** — Describe your component's color values (text color, background color, border color, interactive state colors) and ask Claude to evaluate contrast ratios against WCAG requirements. Minimum 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text and UI components. Claude can suggest alternative colors that maintain your brand palette while meeting requirements.
• **Keyboard interaction audit** — Describe how your component works with mouse interactions and ask Claude to define the equivalent keyboard interactions. Every mouse action needs a keyboard equivalent: click maps to Enter or Space, hover states need focus equivalents, drag-and-drop needs arrow key or alternative input methods.
• **Screen reader compatibility** — Describe your component's visual design and ask Claude to specify the ARIA roles, labels, and live region behavior it needs. "This component shows a real-time updating status indicator: a colored dot (green/yellow/red) with a text label. What ARIA attributes does it need so screen reader users get the same information as sighted users?"
• **Focus management** — For complex components like modals, drawers, and dropdown menus, ask Claude to specify the focus management behavior. Where does focus go when the component opens? What happens when the user tabs past the last focusable element? Where does focus return when the component closes?

**Pattern-Level Accessibility Review**

Individual components can be accessible in isolation but create accessibility problems in combination:

• **Page-level landmark structure** — Describe a page layout composed of your design system components and ask Claude to evaluate the landmark structure. Does the page have a single main landmark? Are navigation regions labeled? Are complementary regions (sidebars) properly identified?
• **Heading hierarchy** — Provide a page layout with all its headings and ask Claude to evaluate the heading hierarchy. Are headings in logical order (h1, h2, h3 without skipping levels)? Does every section have a heading? Are heading levels used semantically (for structure) rather than visually (for size)?
• **Error handling accessibility** — Describe your form error pattern and ask Claude to evaluate it for screen reader users. Are errors announced when they appear? Can users navigate from an error message to the field it relates to? Is the error state communicated through more than just color?

**Building Accessibility into the Design System Process**

The most effective approach is integrating accessibility checks into every stage:

• **Design review checklists** — Ask Claude to generate an accessibility checklist specific to your design system. This checklist should cover the most common WCAG violations for each component type and be usable by designers without deep accessibility expertise.
• **Automated + manual testing guidance** — For each component, ask Claude to specify which accessibility aspects can be tested automatically (contrast ratios, ARIA attributes, heading structure) and which require manual testing (keyboard usability, screen reader experience, cognitive load). This helps teams allocate testing effort realistically.
• **Accessibility documentation templates** — For each component's documentation page, include an accessibility section. Ask Claude to generate a template that covers: supported keyboard interactions, ARIA attributes, focus behavior, and known limitations.

sections.title: Design Token Management and Consistency
sections.content:
Design tokens are the atomic values that define your design system — colors, spacing, typography, shadows, animation timing, and more. They're the bridge between design decisions and code implementation. Claude can help manage, audit, and evolve your design token system to maintain consistency across products and platforms.

**Token Architecture with AI Assistance**

A well-structured token system has layers of abstraction. Claude can help you think through and implement this architecture:

• **Primitive tokens (base layer)** — These are raw values with no semantic meaning: color-blue-500: #3B82F6, spacing-4: 16px, font-size-lg: 18px. Ask Claude to help audit your primitive tokens for completeness: "Here are my color primitives. Are there gaps in the scale? Are the increments between values consistent?"
• **Semantic tokens (meaning layer)** — These map primitives to purposes: color-primary: color-blue-500, spacing-component-padding: spacing-4, font-size-heading: font-size-lg. Ask Claude to review your semantic token names for clarity and consistency: "Are these names clear enough that a new team member would know when to use color-primary vs. color-accent vs. color-highlight?"
• **Component tokens (specific layer)** — These map semantic tokens to specific component contexts: button-background-color: color-primary, card-padding: spacing-component-padding. Ask Claude to identify components that are using primitive tokens directly instead of semantic tokens — these are consistency risks.

**Token Auditing and Consistency**

Over time, token systems drift. New values get added without removing old ones, different teams use different tokens for the same purpose, and naming conventions evolve inconsistently:

• **Usage audit** — Provide Claude with your token list and ask it to identify tokens that appear redundant, tokens with inconsistent naming patterns, and tokens that seem to be missing from the system. "Here are my 200 design tokens. Identify any naming inconsistencies, potential duplicates, and gaps in coverage."
• **Cross-platform consistency** — If your design system supports web, iOS, and Android, ask Claude to compare token values across platforms and flag discrepancies. "Here are my spacing tokens for web (px), iOS (pt), and Android (dp). Are the visual sizes consistent across platforms?"
• **Token deprecation planning** — When you need to remove or replace tokens, ask Claude to help plan the migration. "I want to replace these 5 legacy color tokens with 3 new semantic tokens. Generate a migration guide that shows which old token maps to which new token, and flag any cases where the mapping isn't one-to-one."

**Using Claude for Theme Management**

Design tokens enable theming — light mode, dark mode, high contrast mode, brand variations:

• **Dark mode token generation** — Provide your light mode tokens and ask Claude to generate dark mode equivalents. Claude can apply established dark mode principles: invert luminance relationships while maintaining perceived contrast, adjust saturation for dark backgrounds, and ensure text remains readable. "Here are my light theme color tokens. Generate a dark theme that maintains the same visual hierarchy and brand recognition."
• **High contrast mode** — Ask Claude to generate a high contrast token set that meets WCAG AAA standards (7:1 contrast ratio for text). This is more constrained than AA and requires careful color selection.
• **Theme consistency validation** — After generating theme variants, ask Claude to compare them and flag inconsistencies: "In the light theme, the primary button has higher contrast than the secondary button. In the dark theme, they're almost the same. Is this intentional?"

**Evolving Tokens Over Time**

Design tokens aren't static. As your product evolves, your token system needs to evolve with it:

• **Impact analysis** — Before changing a token value, ask Claude to help assess the impact. "If I change spacing-4 from 16px to 12px, which components will be affected? Based on our component documentation, which components might break with this smaller spacing?"
• **Version management** — Ask Claude to help draft release notes for token updates that explain what changed, why it changed, and what designers and developers need to do. Clear communication prevents the confusion that token changes typically create.

sections.title: Create Design System Documentation with Claude
sections.content: Practice using Claude to generate comprehensive design system documentation for a component.

exercise.prompt:
You're documenting a design system component: an AI Response Card. This component displays AI-generated text content within a product interface (not a chat — think inline AI suggestions, generated summaries, or analysis results).

The component has these features:
- Displays AI-generated text with markdown rendering support
- Shows an "AI Generated" label with a small sparkle icon
- Has three action buttons: Copy, Regenerate, and Dismiss
- Supports three states: Loading (skeleton with shimmer), Streaming (text appearing progressively), and Complete (full content visible)
- Has an optional confidence indicator (High / Medium / Low) shown as a colored badge
- Collapses to a summary with "Show more" for content longer than 150 words
- Includes a subtle feedback mechanism (thumbs up/down) that appears on hover

Create the complete documentation for this component:

1. **Overview** — A 2-3 sentence description of the component's purpose and when to use it.
2. **Usage guidelines** — When to use this component vs. other options (inline text, modals, chat bubbles). Include 3 "Do" examples and 3 "Don't" examples.
3. **Props/Configuration table** — List all configurable properties with name, type, default value, and description. Include at least 8 props.
4. **States documentation** — Describe all component states (loading, streaming, complete, error, collapsed, expanded) with specific guidance on transitions between states.
5. **Accessibility specification** — ARIA roles, keyboard interactions, screen reader announcements, and focus management for this component.
6. **Content guidelines** — Rules for the AI-generated content displayed in this component: maximum length, formatting support, and how to handle different content types (text, lists, code, tables).

Write documentation that a developer could use to implement the component and a designer could use to apply it correctly.
hints: The overview should focus on what makes this component different from a generic card — it's specifically designed for AI-generated content and includes features like streaming, confidence, and feedback that a standard card doesn't need
For the Do/Don't examples, think about common misuse: using this component for human-written content, hiding the AI label, or showing AI responses without any feedback mechanism
The props table should include props for all the features mentioned: content, loading state, streaming state, confidence level, callbacks for actions, feedback handlers, and collapse behavior

The accessibility specification needs to address how the streaming state is communicated to screen readers — continuously announcing new text would be disruptive, but saying nothing means screen reader users don't know content is appearing
exercise.evaluationCriteria: The response should include: (1) A clear, concise overview that positions the component relative to alternatives and establishes when to use it. (2) Usage guidelines with 3 specific Do/Don't pairs that address realistic misuse scenarios — not generic guidelines that apply to any component. (3) A props table with at least 8 properties covering all mentioned features, with accurate types, sensible defaults, and clear descriptions. (4) Complete state documentation covering all 6+ states with specific transition descriptions — not just 'loading transitions to complete' but the visual behavior during the transition. (5) An accessibility specification that addresses the unique challenges of AI content: streaming announcements (using aria-live with appropriate politeness), confidence indicator semantics, and feedback mechanism keyboard access. (6) Content guidelines that address markdown rendering, length handling, and edge cases (what happens with very short or very long AI responses). Deduct points for generic documentation that doesn't address AI-specific concerns, for missing props, or for accessibility specifications that don't handle the streaming state.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
sections.content:
Integrating AI into your design system requires treating AI interactions as first-class design system citizens:

• **AI components are a new category.** Non-deterministic output, variable latency, and uncertainty are fundamentally different from traditional UI states. Design system components for AI features need more variants, more states, and more careful documentation than standard components.

• **Documentation is a design system feature.** Claude dramatically reduces the cost of generating and maintaining component documentation, pattern guidelines, and content templates. The investment in documentation pays off every time a new team member learns the system or a designer makes a usage decision.

• **Accessibility is non-negotiable.** Use Claude to audit components for WCAG compliance, generate keyboard interaction specifications, and create accessibility testing checklists. Building accessibility into the design system prevents teams from shipping inaccessible features.

• **Tokens are the source of truth.** Design tokens ensure consistency across components, platforms, and themes. Use Claude to audit token systems for redundancy and drift, generate theme variants, and plan token migrations when your design language evolves.
